quick_facts.label: Recognised occupational standard
quick_facts.value: India’s Media & Entertainment Skill Council (MESC) lists “Art Director (Set Designing)” as qualification pack MES/Q3102, version 3.0, at NSQF Level 6; the accompanying model curriculum states that this role may also be referred to as a Set Designer.

quick_facts.label: Core purpose
quick_facts.value: The role determines the visual appearance of a set, designs its key elements, and coordinates the construction or execution of that creative vision.

quick_facts.label: Training scope in the MESC curriculum
quick_facts.value: The MESC curriculum for Art Director (Set Design) is 840 notional hours and covers set design, construction planning, commissioning and supervising construction, checking completion before shooting, wrap-up, and workplace health and safety.

quick_facts.label: Relevant advanced study option
quick_facts.value: FTII’s MFA in Cinema specialisation in Art Direction & Production Design is a three-year programme; its stated eligibility includes bachelor’s degrees in architecture, interior design, visual art, design, applied art, painting, sculpture, or related fine-arts fields.

sections.heading: What a Set Designer does
paragraphs: In the Indian MESC occupational framework, the Set Designer is aligned with the “Art Director (Set Designing)” role. The work starts with interpreting the script and creative brief, researching the period, location and visual references, and turning the intended world of the production into hand drawings, graphic designs, CAD drawings or physical models.

The job continues beyond concept art: the designer measures locations and space requirements, prepares a construction plan and set requirements, commissions and briefs the construction team, monitors progress, checks that the set is complete before the shoot, and manages wrap-up or return/disposal of set items. Design decisions must be balanced against schedule and budget constraints.
bullets: Translate scripts and creative briefs into a coherent set concept.
Prepare scale drawings, blueprints, CAD visualisations, models and construction requirements.
Coordinate with the production designer, director, producers, set-construction supervisors and crafts teams.
Review set completion before shooting and address changes needed for set dressing or installation.
Follow workplace health, safety and emergency procedures during construction and production work.

sections.heading: Entry routes from fine arts or interior design
paragraphs: A B.F.A. or a B.Sc. in Interior Design provides a relevant foundation for this node because set design draws on visualisation, spatial design, drawing, materials and construction awareness. A practical route is to build a portfolio that shows script-to-set thinking—research boards, plans, elevations, models, renders and realised projects—and gain supervised credits or assistant experience in theatre, film, television, advertising or live-production work.

For specialised postgraduate preparation, FTII’s Art Direction & Production Design programme explicitly accepts graduates from interior design, visual art, design, applied art and related fine-arts disciplines. Its programme description includes visual arts and aesthetics, production-design methods, budgeting and management, with hands-on support from technicians, carpenters, painters and craftspeople.

The MESC model curriculum’s stated entry prerequisite applies specifically to its Art Director (Set Designing) training programme—not necessarily to every employer: it specifies either a graduate with three years of relevant experience, or an ITI/diploma in craftsman, set designing or set designer with five years of experience, with a minimum age of 22.
bullets: Complete a relevant undergraduate pathway such as fine arts or interior design.
Create a portfolio demonstrating research, visual storytelling, scale, drawing/CAD or model-making, and buildability.
Seek assistant, trainee, theatre-production, art-department or set-work experience to learn on-set workflows.
Consider structured advanced training in art direction and production design where eligible.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The MESC curriculum emphasises a blend of creative, technical and production-management capability. A Set Designer needs to interpret narrative and period/location cues, research references, visualise space, and communicate a final design clearly to creative and production stakeholders.

Technical competence includes measurement and spatial planning; hand drawing, graphic design, CAD and physical models; understanding construction techniques; preparing requirements and plans; and directing or supervising construction activity. The curriculum also identifies collaboration, communication, decision-making, awareness of cost and timelines, and health-and-safety practice as important to the role.
bullets: Script interpretation, visual research and narrative world-building.
Perspective, scale, measurements, plans, elevations, CAD and model-making.
Knowledge of materials, construction methods, installation and set dressing requirements.
Budget and schedule awareness; procurement and logistics coordination.
Clear briefing, collaborative leadership and safety awareness.

sections.heading: Where and how the work is carried out
paragraphs: Set design is collaborative production work rather than solely studio-based design. MESC’s standard places the role in the Media & Entertainment sector under the set-craft occupation, and its work activities involve coordination with production designers, directors, producers, construction supervisors and construction teams.

Work can move between pre-production design and research, location measurement, workshops or construction areas, studio floors and shooting locations. Because the role includes commissioning construction, installation, transport-related instructions, set checks and emergency-risk awareness, safe working practices are part of the occupational standard.
bullets: Pre-production: scripts, research, reference boards, drawings, models and plans.
Build and installation: coordination with carpenters, painters, decorators, props and construction supervisors.
Production: checking readiness and making required changes before filming or performance.
Safety: identifying hazards, following emergency procedures and reporting risks within organisational processes.
